lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — CRE and the "nightmare bacteria"
The CDC, in a 2013 report and then more emphatically in a 2019 update, used a phrase that has stuck: "nightmare bacteria.

" They were specifically referring to the carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae, CRE — the family of gram-negative gut-dwelling bacteria, including E. coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Enterobacter, Citrobacter, and others, that have become resistant to essentially the last reliable class of beta-lactams.
The phrase isn't hyperbole.
CRE bloodstream infections have mortality rates that, in some studies, exceed 50%.
There are patients, right now, for whom we have, essentially, no working antibiotic — not on the formulary, not in the pipeline, not available on compassionate use.
For those patients, we have come back to the pre-antibiotic era.
Let me unpack what CRE actually is.
The Enterobacteriaceae are a family of gram-negative bacilli that live in the gut.
They include all the species I just named.

Many of them cause infections — E. coli causes urinary tract and bloodstream infections, Klebsiella causes pneumonia and bloodstream infections, Enterobacter causes hospital-acquired infections of all kinds.
The carbapenems — meropenem, imipenem, ertapenem — were, for decades, the drugs that worked when nothing else did against these organisms.
CRE means: these bugs have acquired resistance even to the carbapenems.
The mechanism, as we discussed in Segment 4, is typically one of the carbapenemases — KPC, NDM, OXA-48, VIM, IMP — sometimes combined with porin loss and efflux pump upregulation.

The CDC's 2019 Antibiotic Resistance Threats Report classifies CRE as an urgent threat, the highest tier, alongside C. difficile, carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter, drug-resistant Candida auris, and drug-resistant Neisseria gonorrhoeae.
In the US, CRE causes on the order of 13,000 healthcare-associated infections per year, with about 1,100 deaths directly attributed.
Globally, the burden is much higher, particularly in India, China, parts of the Middle East, and Southern Europe, where carbapenem resistance has become endemic in major hospitals.
Why are CRE infections so hard to treat?
Three reasons.

First, the resistance mechanisms — carbapenemases plus other modifications — typically render the organism resistant not just to carbapenems but to most beta-lactams, fluoroquinolones, and aminoglycosides simultaneously, because the same plasmid that carries the carbapenemase often carries half a dozen other resistance genes.
A single mobile element confers multi-drug resistance in one transfer event.
This is the modular-cassette property of plasmid-borne resistance, and we'll see it again in NDM-1.

Second, the remaining drugs that do work — colistin (more on that in Segment 12), tigecycline, fosfomycin, the newer beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations like ceftazidime-avibactam and meropenem-vaborbactam — are either toxic, suboptimal pharmacokinetically, or resistant strains are already emerging.

Third, the patient population that gets CRE infections is, by selection, already sick — hospitalized, often in ICUs, often immunocompromised, often with central lines and ventilators and surgical wounds.
The host can't help fight what the drugs can't kill.
Treatment options for CRE in 2026 are limited and depend on which carbapenemase the organism is producing.
For KPC-producers, ceftazidime-avibactam works — avibactam is a non-beta-lactam beta-lactamase inhibitor that effectively neutralizes KPC.
For NDM-producers, ceftazidime-avibactam does not work because avibactam doesn't inhibit the metallo-beta-lactamases.

For NDM, you might combine ceftazidime-avibactam with aztreonam — aztreonam isn't hydrolyzed by metallo-beta-lactamases, and the avibactam blocks any co-existing ESBL or class A enzymes the bug also carries.

Cefiderocol is a siderophore-conjugated cephalosporin — it tricks the bacterium into actively importing the drug via iron-uptake channels, bypassing porin loss — and it has activity against many CRE including some NDM-producers, though resistance to cefiderocol is already appearing.
Plazomicin is a newer aminoglycoside designed to resist most aminoglycoside-modifying enzymes.
Tigecycline is a glycylcycline derivative of tetracycline, broadly active but with poor blood levels — not the first choice for bloodstream infections.
Colistin — the polymyxin we'll discuss separately in Segment 12 — is genuinely last-resort, with significant nephrotoxicity and emerging resistance.
Notice what this list does.

It is a list of essentially every novel antibacterial drug that has been approved in the last decade, repurposed and combined in increasingly desperate ways to cover an organism that has acquired pre-existing resistance to most of them.
The treatment of a CRE bloodstream infection in 2026 is no longer "give the patient an antibiotic.
" It is "consult infectious diseases, sequence the organism's resistome, pick a combination of two or three drugs based on which enzymes are present, monitor MICs, and hope.
" This is the texture of clinical practice in the era of high-end resistance, and it is qualitatively different from the era when penicillin worked.
CRE is the canonical demonstration of what the WHO calls the post-antibiotic era.
Not for everyone.
Not yet.
But for some patients, in some hospitals, with some infections, on some days, the era of routine bacterial infection being curable with a pill is already over.
Now — how do these bugs share resistance so fast?
Let's go look at the mechanism that explains the speed.
